records.ety: [OE. felawe, felaghe, Icel. fēlagi, fr. fēlag companionship, prop., a laying together of property; fē property + lag a laying, pl. lög law, akin to liggja to lie. See Fee, and Law, Lie to be low.]

defs: A companion; a comrade; an associate; a partner; a sharer.
quotes: We are fellows still, Serving alike in sorrow.
That enormous engine was flanked by two fellows almost of equal magnitude.
notes: ☞ Commonly used of men, but sometimes of women.

defs: A man without good breeding or worth; an ignoble or mean man.
quotes: Worth makes the man, and want of it, the fellow.

defs: An equal in power, rank, character, etc.
quotes: It is impossible that ever Rome Should breed thy fellow.

defs: One of a pair, or of two things used together or suited to each other; a mate; the male.
quotes: When they be but heifers of one year, . . . they are let go to the fellow and breed.
This was my glove; here is the fellow of it.
She seemed to be a good sort of fellow.

defs: In the English universities, a scholar who is appointed to a foundation called a fellowship, which gives a title to certain perquisites and privileges.
In an American college or university, a member of the corporation which manages its business interests; also, a graduate appointed to a fellowship, who receives the income of the foundation.
A member of a literary or scientific society; as, a Fellow of the Royal Society.
quotes: Were the great duke himself here, and would lift up My head to fellow pomp amongst his nobles.
notes: ☞ Fellow is often used in compound words, or adjectively, signifying associate, companion, or sometimes equal. Usually, such compounds or phrases are self-explanatory; as, fellow-citizen, or fellow citizen; fellow-student, or fellow student; fellow-workman, or fellow workman; fellow-mortal, or fellow mortal; fellow-sufferer; bedfellow; playfellow; workfellow.

defs: To suit with; to pair with; to match.
